one_line_takeaway: SelfGrader detects LLM jailbreaks cheaply and robustly by scoring queries on a small numerical logit scale (0-100) calibrated with PAC-guided ICL anchors and combined via a dual maliciousness/benignness scoring rule, beating text- and feature-based guardrail baselines on both attack success rate and latency/memory overhead.
key_finding: On Llama-3-8B-Instruct, averaged across six jailbreak attack families (IJP, GCG, AutoDAN, DrAttack, MultiJail, ActorAttack), SelfGrader achieves ASR 0.11% / PGR 2.51%, the lowest among all practical baselines, with 0.77s latency and 640.15MB memory overhead -- versus e.g. Token Highlighter's 3.34% ASR / 7.55% PGR at 27.78s latency and 29283.44MB memory (SelfGrader is reported as ~36x faster with a small fraction of the memory). Against Perplexity Filter specifically, on the manual IJP attack SelfGrader reduces ASR by 7.80 points and PGR by 98.70 points. QGuard reaches 0% ASR/PGR on all attacks but is flagged as producing near-100% FPR (severe over-refusal) and is excluded from head-to-head 'practical guardrail' comparisons. Ablation (Table 3): removing ICL anchor examples raises ASR from 0.11% to 7.32% and PGR from 2.51% to 70.27%; removing the benignness (DPL) view drops ASR to 0.05% and PGR to 1.16% but pushes FPR up to 29.26%; removing the maliciousness view raises PGR to 48.47% (FPR only 0.17%) -- demonstrating the two DPL components are complementary rather than redundant. Sweeping the number of numerical tokens Q from 2 to 101 lowers average ASR/PGR from 1.51%/11.07% to 0.11%/2.51%; increasing further to Q=1000 gives only marginal further improvement (0.08%/2.17%) at higher latency (0.83s) and memory (660.20MB). Under additional robustness tests (Table 2): Random Search (guardrail-adaptive suffix search) 0.00%/0.00% ASR/PGR; Emoji Attack (token segmentation) 0.36%/2.18%; Prompt Injection Attack 0.00%/5.33%; FITD (multi-step) 0.00%/0.00% -- all improving over Llama Guard (Pre) and SelfDefend (Direct) baselines shown in the same table.
core_contribution: SelfGrader, a lightweight LLM jailbreak guardrail that reframes malicious-query detection as a numerical grading problem: instead of relying on generated text, keyword lists, or internal-hidden-state features, it prompts the (target or an auxiliary) guardrail LLM to 'grade' a user query using a compact set of Q ordinal numerical tokens (NTs, e.g. 0-9, or Q=101 by default) and reads the logits over just those NT positions as an internal safety signal. Three components: (1) NT-based logit extraction, using a policy-conditioned system prompt built from a harm-category taxonomy (Cpolicy) plus PAC-guided in-context-learning (ICL) anchor examples that calibrate the NT scale to the target safety rubric, with a proved calibration bound (Theorem 1) on the gap between the NT-logit score and an oracle maliciousness score; (2) a dual-perspective logit (DPL) scoring rule that computes both a maliciousness score s(+) and a benignness score s(-) from two separate prompts, combines them via sDPL = lambda*s(+) + (1-lambda)*(Q-s(-)-1) with lambda=0.5 by default, and applies top-k tail trimming (k=0.2Q) to the softmax-normalized NT logits before aggregation to reduce noise from low-probability tokens; (3) a threshold decision rule tau_D=(Q-1)/2 that blocks or forwards the query.
threat_model: Defender-side guardrail sitting as a pre-processing filter in front of a target LLM. Adversary can be black-box (API access, observes only text outputs) or white-box (access to logits/gradients), and may act adaptively over up to E interaction rounds, refining a query P to make the target LLM produce output y satisfying a fixed jailbreak goal G, judged successful by an external safety judge J_G. The guardrail (parameterized by theta_D, which may be the target model itself or a separate auxiliary LLM) has inference-time access to its own token-level logits but not to gradients or hidden states, and must operate under a practical latency/memory budget. Defender objective is formalized as minimizing the sum of attack success probability on adversarial queries plus false-positive rate on benign queries.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states: SelfGrader is evaluated on a broad but finite range of jailbreak attacks, target LLMs, and safety scenarios, so future/unseen attacks or deployment settings may behave differently; and because calibration anchors are built from a general safety rubric, applying SelfGrader to substantially different content policies would require reconstructing the rubric and ICL anchor examples. Additionally, from the reported results: QGuard's near-0% ASR/PGR shows a pure ASR/PGR comparison can be misleading without jointly considering FPR, and the method's default configuration (Q=101, k=0.2Q) was tuned on the evaluated benchmark suite, so generalization of these exact hyperparameters to new rubrics/models is not established beyond the ablation sweeps shown.

quantitative_results: SelfGrader average ASR 0.11% / PGR 2.51% across six jailbreak attacks on Llama-3-8B-Instruct
SelfGrader latency 0.77s, memory overhead 640.15MB vs Token Highlighter 27.78s / 29283.44MB
SelfGrader reduces ASR by 7.80 points and PGR by 98.70 points vs Perplexity Filter on IJP attack
Ablation: removing ICL anchors raises ASR 0.11%->7.32%, PGR 2.51%->70.27%
Ablation: removing benign view drops ASR to 0.05%/PGR 1.16% but raises FPR to 29.26%
Ablation: removing malicious view raises PGR to 48.47% (FPR 0.17%)
Varying Q from 2 to 101 reduces average ASR/PGR from 1.51%/11.07% to 0.11%/2.51%; Q=1000 gives 0.08%/2.17% at 0.83s/660.20MB
Robustness table: Random Search 0.00%/0.00%, Emoji Attack 0.36%/2.18%, Prompt Injection Attack 0.00%/5.33%, FITD 0.00%/0.00% ASR/PGR
